Title: Comment from Goyette, Clare
Comment:
I have been a Care Credit customer for close to 2 decades.  It was first introduced to me by my dentist.  The use of Care Credit allowed me to have much needed dental work and pay for it over time.  

Cared Credit helped me cover almost $5000 worth of surgeries and treatments for my Boxer.  I have successfully used the product by paying as agreed and in full at the conclusion of the term. 
Recently, I had to use Care Credit for $1250 at my Vet. My little sick kitty had to have a number of tests, blood work and ultrasound.  At the end, he was very sick and I had to put him to sleep.  I could use Care Credit or any other credit card, but chose Care Credit for the option of a fixed payment. 

Why is Care Credit any different than Rooms to Go, Home Depot, Lowes, Best Buy or any other business that offers a Zero Interest program for a fixed monthly payment and fixed period.  

Read the fine print and use the product(s) appropriately.

Title: Comment from Brosseau, Spencer
Comment: A common way to pay for qualified medical expenses is through an employers Flexible Spending Account (FSA). This allows employees to contribute tax free to an account, usually administered by a third-party, and then use those funds to pay for qualified medical expenses. A significant issue that is common amongst Flexible Spending Accounts is that many have a use-it-or-lose-it provision and funds contributed that are not used by the end of a certain time period, typically the end of a plan year, are forfeited. This is a disincentive for people to use Flexible Spending Accounts for health related problems because most people are not able to forecast medical issues unless they are recurring. It would be beneficial to review this policy in the case of medical issues to help more people reduce the burden of unexpected medical costs. Many Health Savings Accounts (HSA) already allow yearly carry over so it would make sense to have similar expectations for Flexible Spending Accounts.

Title: Comment from Anonymous
Comment: I have been using CareCredit for a little over a year, since late June, 2022 and honestly wish I had never gotten involved. I felt pressured into signing up for CareCredit by an emergency vet clinic, before diagnostics were completed and I was informed my pet had extremely limited chances of recovery. I ended up with a debt of a little under $5,000. There was an “interest free” period of only 6 months, but it ended up not mattering at all, as in 7 months, I have been charged over $1,300 in interest. I felt as if I’ve been taken advantage of by Synchronity bank, the entity that issues this card. I would not have signed up for such a card if I hadn’t been in the emotional state I was in, and very much feel taken advantage of by the issuing bank.

Title: Comment from Powell, Rebecca
Comment: This is another example of why we need universal healthcare in the United States. Our current system allows prices to skyrocket (we spend more than any other developed country on healthcare costs, and for really relatively little benefit), and most bankruptcies are related to medical debt. This new practice of just signing patients up on admittance into finance contracts will put more people in financial jeopardy, and hospitals signing contracts with for-profit finance companies makes me very uncomfortable. I disapprove of this interest-bearing financing of medical bills.

Title: Comment from Unell, Alan
Comment:
If there were universal healthcare in the US we would not be having a discussion on financing health care debt at the point of service.  Most bankruptcies in the US are related to medical debt.  This predatory activity of pushing patients into finance contracts will only make matters worse for patients and their families and increase the number of bankruptcies.  Hospitals used to set up payment plans when needed, often at no interest, because they had the appearance of operating for the public good.

I strongly disapprove of this interest bearing financing of medical bills

Title: Comment from Koerner, Kenny
Comment: There are many different types of medical payment products. I'd be careful to lump them all into the same category.  My organization partners with a local bank to provide financing to our patients for up to 72 months at an interest rate of between 4%-6%. Offering the financing to our patients is not our first option by any means, it is just there as another option for our patients in addition to applying for financial assistance, making no interest payments that based upon patient balance run for up to 24 months in length, and then as a final option to offer, the financing for up to 72 months.  Since Medicare and Medicaid and now Medicare Advantage don't even pay hospitals and organizations the cost of providing services, it is imperative that hospitals are able to collect patient due amount portion of the healthcare services provided. I am asking that all forms of medical payment products are highlighted such as the product we are using at our organization.

Title: Comment from Noreen, Kristin
Comment:
In 2010 (13 years ago today, in fact), I was hit by a car on my bike at age 46. I had 15 fractures, a traumatic brain injury, internal organ damage and an amputated hand (with successful reattachment). I was treated at PeaceHealth St. Joseph Hospital in Bellingham, Washington, for 8 weeks, during which my bills were over $1 million. Those were covered by insurances and a charitable grant, all but $9,000, which came out of my pocket. The kicker, though, was aftercare. I will need chiropractic, massage and acupuncture care for the rest of my life. These things are minimally, if at all, covered by insurance. The only way to pay for these uncovered services is by credit card if I don't have the money up front, and I usually don't, because I can only work part time and I don't qualify for disability. My income is $25,000 a year, I pay $7,000 in self-employment tax, and my credit cards currently have a $12,000 balance, which is all medical debt. I pay about $200 a month in interest on this balance, and there's no end in sight. No ship coming in. No mercy if I can't make my payments. After all, Capital One doesn't care if I spent the money getting blowouts at Gene Juarez or getting a dislocated rib popped back in place. 
I filed bankruptcy in 2016 to discharge $56,000 in medical debt. I lost my condominium too, and I now live in a tiny house on wheels in someone's backyard. 
There needs to be protection for people who are backed into this corner, or better yet, we shouldn't have to pay out of our own pockets for these services. Medical debt is crushing people.

Title: Comment from Anonymous
Comment:
I am writing to express my extreme dissatisfaction with the services provided by Care Credit. My initial encounter with the company was during a dentist appointment when I was experiencing tooth pain. They bated me and painted the card like it was perfect for me.
However, my experience since then has been nothing short of disappointing. Not only has my balance of $700 not significantly decreased despite making consistent and timely payments, but it has actually risen to $638 over the course of 10+ months. Even though I sometimes pay more than the minimum due, the balance inexplicably continues to climb.
What's more concerning is the feeling that I can no longer trust a company that pushes their credit card on individuals, seemingly prioritizing profit over customer satisfaction. This loss of trust was solidified when I opted to forgo a recommended dental treatment during a subsequent appointment simply because I couldn't trust a company that operates in such a manner.
I am sharing this experience in the hope that others will be cautious when considering Care Credit's services. My intention is not only to vent my frustration but also to shed light on the less-than-ideal practices that seem to be the norm for this company. Transparent and fair financial solutions should be at the heart of any reputable business, and unfortunately, I found that lacking in my dealings with Care Credit.

Title: Comment from Anonymous
Comment: So far I've been fortunate enough not to fall into medical debt. However, the cost of health insurance is so ridiculously high that in the past I've gone for several years without insurance and just prayed that I wouldn't get into a car accident or be diagnosed with a disease like cancer. Even with insurance, I don't always go to my doctor when it would probably be a good idea because my deductible is a few thousand dollars. Now that my husband and I are newly retired and likely to need more medical care in the coming years, we're seriously considering moving to another country that has excellent medical care for a reasonable cost. It would be an absolute nightmare to be elderly and bankrupted by our ludicrous medical system, particularly since my husband and I have no children who would be able to help care for us if we ran out of money.

Title: Comment from Verret, Jude
Comment:
I am writing to express my sincere concern and request regarding the availability of deferred payment options on credit cards. As a responsible credit card user, I believe that these options play a crucial role in providing financial flexibility and security to consumers like me.

Deferred payment plans provide a significant level of flexibility and financial relief to individuals facing unexpected expenses, emergencies, or temporary financial hardships. Such options help consumers manage their finances more effectively, preventing them from falling into undue stress or potential debt traps. It is my belief that allowing these options to remain accessible will greatly contribute to the overall well-being of consumers.

I routinely utilize deferred interest options for medical procedures that health insurance does not cover or necessary veterinary services. If it would not be for deferred interest options, I would be forced to utilize standard credit card terms that include exorbitant interest rates.

I understand the importance of responsible lending practices and the need to ensure consumers are well-informed about the terms and conditions of deferred payment plans. However, I would like to emphasize that with appropriate transparency and clear communication, these options can continue to benefit both consumers and financial institutions alike.

In conclusion, I kindly urge the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau to consider the positive impact that deferred payment options have on consumers' financial stability. I believe that maintaining these options aligns with the principles of consumer protection and empowerment.

Thank you for your time and consideration of this matter.

Jude Verret

Title: Comment from Colorado Children's Campaign
Comment:
My name is Hunter Nelson, and I am a Senior Policy Analyst at the Colorado Children’s Campaign. Together with our partner organizations and communities across the state, we advocate for the development and implementation of data-driven public policy that improves child and family well-being. We fight for a world in which, without exception, public policies and investments remove barriers for most impacted children and families and improve well-being for every child and every family. The Children’s Campaign recognizes the devastating impacts of medical debt on families and is in strong support of eliminating predatory medical loan practices that negatively impact families’ economic stability. 

Medical debt is a major problem in Colorado. According to the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (2022), approximately 1 in 8 Coloradans hold medical debt in collections, amounting to a total sum of more than $1.3 million. Medical debt also disproportionately impacts households with children – according to the Census Bureau, households with children under the age of 18 (24.7%) were more likely to have medical debt than households without children (16.5%).  Birthing people are also more likely to have medical debt. Women are more likely to report having medical debt than men, and 1 in 8 American households report that pregnancy and/or birth costs contributed to their medical debt.  

Medical debt can have crippling impacts on families with children, causing families to forgo essential needs such as food, clothing and rental payments, spending down their savings to pay for medical bills, borrowing money from family members or friends, and taking on additional debts.  Also, when medical debt is included in consumer reports, which are frequently pulled by employers and landlords and can significantly impact the decision on whether to offer employment or housing to an applicant. The inclusion of medical debt on these reports can negatively impact a family‘s credit score, which can have rippling effects on subsequent employment and housing opportunities. 

According to a 2020 AIMS Public Health report, people with medical debt are three times more likely to have mental health symptoms such as depression, anxiety and stress. Not having to worry about medical debt and its inclusion on consumer reports has the potential to promote better mental and emotional health outcomes among families nationwide.

The Children’s Campaign believes that every family with children in Colorado and nationwide should have the resources and opportunities to achieve economic prosperity, and eliminating predatory medical loan practices that cause families to accrue enormous amounts of medical debt is one step toward making this a reality for all.  

Thank you,

Hunter Nelson
Senior Policy Analyst 
Hunter@coloradokids.org 

i. Medical Debt Burden in the United States (consumerfinance.gov) 
ii. Who Had Medical Debt in the United States? (census.gov)
iii. The burden of medical debt in the United States - Peterson-KFF Health System Tracker
iv. Ibid. 
v. Problems paying medical bills and mental health symptoms post-Affordable Care Act - PMC (nih.gov)

Title: Comment from Fisher, Jacob
Comment:
I went to the ER of Seton Medical Center Harker Heights in May 2022when I was having heart palpitations. I figured it was from caffeine, but wanted to be safe with a family history of heart disease. I told the financial lady I would only pay the Medicare price for the visit. One month later I was billed $2,664 for an EKG and some labs. The Medicare price for an identical bill is $203. They billed it as a level 4 ER visit which is upcoding. They billed me for additional codes outside of the ER visit code which is code unbundling. I disputed the bill and sent a letter to the hospital, but they still sent me to debt collections. I disputed it to the first debt collections company and they dropped it. It was sent to a second debt collections company that reports to bureaus (damages credit scores). I disputed it and they dropped it months later. Currently it is with a third debt collections agency, which does not report to the credit agencies. Although, there is no threat to my credit score, I am sending a final dispute letter before I take the hospital to small claims court to settle this.

I work in health insurance and patient advocacy, and have disputed bills for others as well. In general, hospitals do not inform patients of their 501r financial assistance policy thresholds, when the patient was eligible for a 100% discount. Hospitals bill patient’s significantly higher prices than Medicare for identical services. 

The photo below does not represent the Medicare price for this bill, but rather the difference of prices for each code if they were billed separately.

Title: Comment from Silva, Eva
Comment: No one should feel like they would rather risk their health, their life at times, just because they're unfamiliar with the healthcare system. It is unconscionable that healthcare admins/non-profit hospitals, especially those who are subsidized specifically to assist people below the Federal Poverty Level (who are likeliest to be least informed about how healthcare systems work) withhold VITAL information from their charges. It is necessary to broaden the public's knowledge with regard to their own health and the access they have a right to. The current systems, all systems, are arbitrary and instead of working toward the collective good, these supposed non-profts are actively harming the communities they claim to serve, by keeping us all in the dark.

Title: Comment from Lee, Leona
Comment: Hello, I've lived in Cook County, IL for over 40 years. I've always had insurance, and lately also Medicare & Medicaid- yet still, there have been several times where I suddenly got a letter from a collections agency or it showed up on my credit report, that I suddenly owe a co-pay for a test or surgery. I've had sudden collection demands for $400, a co-pay from Advocate Christ Hospital for spinal surgery in 2008. Also: several hundred from an X-ray co-pay I did not even know existed, at Illinois Masonic Hospital in Lakeview.  And, $1000 for Occupational Therapy when I had to get a finger amputated in 2020. I have gone to & called the hospital's financial offices, where I was told I HAD to make payments. I am disabled & get SSDI, so I have no wages to garnish. I'm disabled, on a fixed income, and cannot work, yet I was told I HAD to make payments, in one case I paid off half & they STILL sent it to collections!  This is ridiculous. I need dental work, and I need both knees replaced- but I have put things off because I am afraid of medical bills, afraid of anything else going to collections, and I will not be able to find my next apt because these things are on my credit report history!

Title: Comment from Vernier, Jane Rhea
Comment: Two years ago I was directed to go to the ER 2ce within the span of a month my more than one doctor, and I'll be paying for it for the next 3 years. I have insurance, and my bill was still out of control. Neither hospital, nor any clinic I've been to since has informed me of financial aid options. Some hospitals don't have auto-pay set up in their portal, so if I miss a deadline once because of my Neurodiversity, I'm back on the chopping block with collections. Some hospitals don't have a clinic for a specialty I need, so they give me a diagnosis, and then send me to another doctor who fear mongers me into procedures I don't need, and really can't afford. I get ads all the time for CreditCare, but that has interest, and medical bills should not have interest. Why should there be a pay wall to my life or death?

The federal government needs to act urgently to protect the wellbeing of everyday Americans from predatory hospital systems that refuse to offer programs they are legally mandated to provide.

And you get a pretty high interest rate on top of a high bill. At the very, very least, our regulators should require that these products stay within the guidelines and, more importantly, that prices for the services match Medicare levels. Title: Comment from Devine, Paul
Comment:
I have had the experience of needing high-cost dental care. This care was not affordable for me but I was told I could get financing. Them financing interest was greatly bloated. I feel the dental practitioner may have received a "kickback" for pushing me in the direction of a particular loan agency. Where the need for financing to overcome health issues is fair, it may be better to used a standard credit card if a great enough credit limit can be established.
Better still is to provide a government loan system which may have more avenues to pay back with lower interest if any at all. Guaranteed health care for all, even if it requires a loan-type instrument, should include every type of care including dental, eye, etc.

It would be in the best interest to all if the CFPB would recommend such a system.

Title: Comment from McClung, Nell
Comment: Credit Card Companies such as Credit One had a third party offer disability insurance to insure/pay for the monthly installments if a claim is filed and approved.  However, the third party solicited the insurance coverage when I informed them, I was on Medicare Disability.  That third party charged Credit One since 2010 for disability premiums. I filed a claim in 2019, I was told I did not qualify because I was disabled.  I contacted Credit One and was told they would refund the premiums for the last 10 years.  They only refunded the premiums for maybe the last 5 years.  I requested that they credit the interest charged on those illegally charged premium.  This was a difficult procedure since all credit card companies have customer service centers in India, China or other Third World Countries.  Those agents do not know the intricacies of accounting as it relates to billing.  I was transferred 5 times, spent over an hour on the phone. I sent Faxes and followed up with calls.  I went through the same series with different agents EVERY time I called.  They needed a supervisor.  The Supervisor needs a supervisor.  This is not how America use to do business before technology got involved with Customer Service.  We have complaints about immigration, but Congress allows Corporations to hire immigrants from other Countries to man their Customer Service Centers knowing they are under qualified and cannot speak or understand English well. Consumers just give up because of the red tape. It's a win for Corporations because they have very low labor cost and Consumers incur interest from these disputed claims or purchases. Banks hold on to the money which is making interest on the money which they do not legally own. Credit One refused to send me an itemize list of the disability charges and interest, as well as the contact information for their Third-Party Partner.  Credit One ended up selling my debt to Resurgent in 2020. I contacted them by phone and fax to tell them why I am not paying the bill.  I disputed the balance. Resurgent only sent a copy of the statement from the last time Credit One billed me as proof I owed them the debt.  I disagreed with them and stated that I need the itemized charges and interest related to the disability premiums over those 10 years.  They responded they do not have it.  I responded they have failed to prove I owe the debt.  I have this as a collection item on my credit report.  Banks are refusing to answer these disputes.  CFPB Complaints, which I have submitted, for Credit One and Resurgent, have not made any progress in resolving this issue.  Banks and Corporations are clearly aware that the CFPB does not do anything after a response is sent.  It is a waste of time if you do not implement some type of regulation which requires them to address the issue timely or per the FDCPA Rule regarding ownership of the debt is not established and the Consumer can sue to recover based on that regulation.  This should be across the board for every bank. They affect millions of consumers. Medical Credit Cards are another step in predatory lending and pushed on patients when they are in need of medical assistance.  There should be a limit on the interest rate that can be charged and repayment term.  I suspect that these Medical Credit Cards pays kickbacks to the Medical Facility that offer them.  This is also predatory, and the hardship is on the poor, seniors, disabled and working-class poor. We are living in an era of "Unchecked Capitalism" since the 1990's deregulations of ownership rules and initial regulations barred this type of predatory profiteering and economic discrimination. The 1996 FCC approval of Opinionated Broadcasting/Publishing has made it very difficult to get true information regarding what Consumer are getting.  Congress makes laws for the Corporations and Billionaires and no longer make laws for the betterment of the Citizen/Consumers of America.  This needs to change.

Title: Comment from Keffer, Tricia
Comment: Good evening, Short, sweet, and simple story. I had a full benefits job. I left that job to work under a registered landscape architecture because - the Board of Landscape Architecture will ONLY accept the one year of experience under a registered landscape architect to get my Florida title and license. I didn't need to leave that job and the benefits. It should have counted for my one year. I drove myself into the hospital via 6 weeks of round trips from Key Largo to Miami for that job. The stress and inflammation took a terrible toll on my body. The driving was almost 6 hours per day. To add insult to injury, I was bullied, made fun, and harassed by hospital staff and doctors at South Miami Baptist. I was forcible castrated screaming into the surgery mask to stop. I'm really only allowed to say the medical tests are disputed. I suspect the hospital staff are under great pressure to meet quotas. Even if it's only "unofficial". That's why we MUST have universal health care. For the wealthiest country in the world to not offer medical care is absurd. We are the laughing stock when other less wealthy countries can do it. If I had gone to the hospital earlier, I would have been in a better state of mind. The cost would have been lower and the outcome more positive. It is fair to say at this point, I loath doctors. The fear of medical debt with suddenly no insurance is frightening. How could you do that to me! How can this administration not fight tooth and nail for health care. Why? I'd like to know. If people who would vote for former President Trump would also vote for Bernie Sanders, then it's not about party. It's about desperation. Will the Dems show up for us? I don't know. Show me - don't tell me.

Title: Comment from Human Rights Watch
Comment:
Hello,

Please find attached to this comment a submission from Human Rights Watch to the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB), Department of Health and Human Services (HHS), and Department of the Treasury (Treasury) (collectively, the “agencies”), in response to their July 12, 2023 “Request for Information Regarding Medical Payment Products.”  

Human Rights Watch strongly supports the agencies’ efforts to better regulate the rights-impacting practices of private entities involved in the healthcare system and in the collection of medical debt. This submission draws from our research in the US to respond to the agencies’ calls for additional information, particularly outlining debt-producing practices of tax-exempt hospital organizations that should be regulated to improve access to health care.

Thank you for this opportunity to provide comment. We would be happy to provide additional information, and also welcome the opportunity to further discuss these issues and our research. If you would like to arrange such a meeting, please contact Matt McConnell (mcconnm@hrw.org) and Jack Spehn (spehnj@hrw.org).

Much Appreciated, 
Matt McConnell

Researcher, Human Rights Watch
Economic Justice & Rights Division
mcconnm@hrw.org

Title: Comment from Foley, Ian
Comment: The only medical credit card I've ever been pitched was Care Credit through Synchrony, pitched by my optholmology provider at the time of my cataract surgery.  My health care provider only covered the removal of the cataract and a blank (non-RX) lens.  I had to pay for the prescription lens, which struck me as odd.  So Care Credit offered a promo for 0% financiing for 24 months, but then an interest rate of 22.9% and above, with severe penalty APR if a payment was missed, late, etc.  I have also had clients who accepted similar credit offers, only to find themselves in deeper and deeper consumer credit debt.  It seems a medical provider would better serve a patient by offering in-house monthly payments, made directly to them, rather than a nationally based credit card company with no compassion or personal interest in the patient.

Title: Comment from Willette, Trish
Comment:
The issues surrounding medical debt and healthcare costs in America are complex and interconnected, often driven by a combination of factors. Here's an overview of some of the key factors contributing to the medical debt crisis in the United States:

High Healthcare Costs: One of the primary drivers of medical debt in America is the exorbitant cost of healthcare services. The U.S. has some of the highest healthcare costs in the world, which can lead to significant financial burdens on patients, even those with insurance.

Confusing Medical Billing: The healthcare billing process in the U.S. is notoriously complex and confusing. Patients often receive bills that are difficult to decipher, leading to unexpected charges and disputes. This lack of transparency can result in patients accruing medical debt unintentionally.

Medical Credit Cards: Some patients turn to medical credit cards or high-interest financing options to cover medical expenses. While these cards can provide short-term relief, they often come with steep interest rates, making it easy for patients to accumulate debt.

Lack of Awareness About Assistance Programs: Many patients, particularly those from low-income households, may not be aware of the existence of non-profit hospitals and charitable organizations that offer financial assistance or discounts including the non-hospital system's own financial assistance programs that could make the debt down to zero amount. This lack of awareness can lead to missed opportunities for debt relief.

Rural Hospital Closures: The closure of rural hospitals, often due to financial pressures of large hospital conglomerate like BALLAD Health in rural Northeastern Tennessee, has have a significant impact on communities. Patients in rural areas may face longer travel distances to access care, which can result in increased costs and reduced access to healthcare services. Additionally, death rates from birth to aged will increase as they were in in the early 1900s to mid 1900s.

Healthcare Conglomerates: Non-profit corporate healthcare conglomerates can acquire and manage multiple healthcare facilities, including rural hospitals. The financial decisions made by these conglomerates can sometimes prioritize profits over community access to care, leading to closures and reduced or no healthcare services.
Ballad health is in an effort to close small rural healthcare facilities five years after buying them up.

Addressing the medical debt crisis in America requires comprehensive reform of the healthcare system, increased transparency in medical billing, better patient education, and policies aimed at reducing the overall cost of healthcare services. Additionally, efforts to expand access to affordable healthcare, especially in underserved areas, are crucial to mitigating the impact of medical debt on vulnerable populations.

Title: Comment from Ferrarie, Anna
Comment: I consider myself lucky that I don't have personal experience with medical debt, but through friends' and acquaintances' experiences I am shocked by how quickly medical debt is sent to collection agencies even by "nonprofit" hospitals. Medical expenses are not discretionary spending. Debt from life saving care should be treated differently than other debit. Stress caused by collection calls/unpaid bills is the least conducive thing to recovery from illness or injury. Nonprofit hospitals who exploit poor patients by not telling them about their right to free or reduced cost care should be penalized.

Title: Comment from Anonymous
Comment:
Medical debt is a glaring manifestation of the deeply flawed and corrupt health care system in our country. It is an egregious injustice that leaves countless individuals drowning in an insurmountable burden, jeopardizing their financial stability and overall well-being. The exorbitant costs associated with medical treatments have created a vicious cycle of crippling debt for patients. This dire situation demands immediate attention and concrete actions to rectify the systemic issues plaguing our health care system. It is imperative to implement comprehensive reforms that prioritize affordability, transparency, and accessibility of medical services for all citizens.

Hold medical debt credit card and installment companies accountable! It is time to challenge the unethical practices that have plagued the healthcare industry for far too long. These financial institutions have profited off the vulnerability and desperation of patients in need of critical medical treatment. Charging exorbitant interest rates and fees, they trap individuals in an unending cycle of debt. It is outrageous that people could be burdened with staggering bills while already grappling with illness. The lack of regulation and oversight has enabled these predatory companies to exploit patients without consequence. It is high time we demand justice for those affected by their ruthless tactics. We need strict regulations that limit interest rates, prohibit hidden charges, and ensure transparent billing practices. Moreover, we must hold these companies accountable through legal action when they engage in deceptive practices or harass patients who are simply seeking care. Empathy and compassion should be the driving force behind our healthcare system, not greed at the expense of vulnerable individuals' well-being.

By tackling corruption within the industry, holding insurance companies accountable for unjust practices, and promoting universal access to quality health care, we can begin to alleviate the burdensome weight of medical debt on individuals and families across the nation. Effective solutions must be implemented swiftly as every day that passes without real change deepens the suffering caused by this prevalent issue.

Title: Comment from Browne, James
Comment: In 2006 my Dr. had me go to the first not for profit Hosp for tests. After almost a year and many different expensive test they gave me my diagnosis of stage 4 cancer and a very glim prognosis. I then went to a different not for profit hosp.for a second opinion. The Dr's there were puzzled why they conducted so many "unnecessary" test when the last test they did held all the info required for a proper diagnosis, and should have been the first test. I believe they took advantage of me and my insurance, which covered about 2/3 of the cost, the remainder I had to put on high interest credit cards.The 2nd not for profit hosp gave me a much better prognosis, and after 2 very extensive surgeries, radiation and chemo therapy's  I was cancer free after about 3 years. During this time I was also experiencing 2 very serious and extremely emotional, and stressful personal issues, which were also causing a great deal of financial distress. It was very difficult for me to fully concentrate on the almost impossible to understand hosp, and insurance billing both of which use their own coding and descriptive language. Because of the extreme nature of my cancer I had to do frequent follow ups with the 3 medical departments that treated me, as well as 4 other dept's that need to monitor other areas of my body that could become affected by the orig. cancer and or the therapy's I undertook. I was very happy with the excellent medical support I was receiving but it was all happening very fast and I was being ushered around the hosp for test's and no one was informing me or counseling me on what the costs for these appointments and tests were. Being so frightened of my experience I just followed all orders, and when I did ask questions about cost all the Dr's said I have no idea, that's not my responsibility, and offered no suggestions. During this time I received 2 skin cancer diagnosis which required outpatient surgeries. My medical bills were mounting very quickly. Over the course of all the testing and treatments my insurance changed 3 times. Each one had completely different rules on filing, billing, and coverage. I turned to the hosp. billing dept and they were no help, in fact they confused me even more. I applied for financial assistance but did not qualify because I earned to much. At one point after my fist surgery I was losing my house and part of my retirement in a contentious divorce, and my lawyers had me file for bankruptcy. The first surgery and all the money I owed for testing and follow ups were dismissed in bankruptcy, but  2 years later I had accumulated almost $80,000 more in out of pocket costs, and the hosp was threatening to put me in collections so I negotiated a payment plan with the minimum monthly payment of $200. however my medical expenses were out growing my bal. and I had to re negotiate the monthly payment plan up to $250. all the while I tried to have the hospital and insurance companies help me find errors in billing and coverage responsibilities, all to no very frustrating avial. This debt is still following me for 10 years. In Dec 2022 I was diagnosed with stage 4 metastasized prostate cancer, which was causing my kidneys to shut down and putting pressure on nerves that caused me to lose mobility.The treatment plan was swift and  extensive. I had just signed up for medicare and luckily had been advised by a very good insurance person to purchase a good supplemental policy. Which has been a blessing, but I did have to retire earlier than I was planning. I'm grateful for the excellent coverage I now have and I've been able to stay current on my out of pocket expenses. One of the new drugs that are helping to keep me alive has an out of pocket cost of $3000 per month, but I was able to get on a program from the manufacturer to receive it for free. but I'm now on a modest fixed income. I have managed to whittle down my old medical bills to half, but the $250 per month takes a big bite out of my income. I'm positive there are billing errors and over charges as well as insurance responsibility's not meet, but I still struggle to make sence of it all, the original insurance company has gone out of business and the other 2 have been no help, especially that I'm no longer a client, and I still get no help form the billing dept. in fact over the years they have redirected my payments to the wrong account and tried to make that cause for sending me to collections. My saving grace was receipts from my bank stating all payments made on time to the correct account. I'm grateful to the hosp and Dr's for saving my life, but the extreme costs, poor billing, and insurance practices, and lack of clarity and transparency can be as life threatening and debilitating as the diseases and conditions they treat. Thank you for the opportunity to communicate my story and I hope changes can be made to this very important, but flawed and corrupt system.

Title: Comment from Thomas, Glenn
Comment: Although my wife and I have good health insurance through my employer, we have difficulty paying copays and deductibles. Our policy has a $5,000 per year out-of-pocket maximum in addition to the portion we pay for our premium, and this feels more like an out-of-pocket expectation or obligation in order to get health care. We are both have chronic health conditions, and we end up meeting the $5,000 maximum. Our gross income last year was $63,000, so $5,000 makes a difference to our household budget. We’ve had to delay and put off necessary expenses to our home and vehicle in order to make sure we can make medical deductibles and copays without going into debt. I work for a public school district and I am represented by the SEIU union. The district’s budget is constrained by politics and the whims of voters when levy referenda are on the ballot. Because of this, I worry about having our insurance coverage reduced or changed to a high deductible policy with every two year contract negotiation.

Title: Comment from Lish, Christopher
Comment:
Sunday, September 10, 2023

Comment Intake—Request for Information Regarding Medical Payment Products
Consumer Financial Protection Bureau
1700 G Street NW,
Washington, DC 20552

Subject: Ban Predatory Medical Payment Products -- Request for Information Regarding Medical Payment Products (Document ID: CFPB-2023-0038-0001)

To Department of the Treasury Deputy Assistant Secretary for Tax Policy Thomas C. West Jr., Consumer Financial Protection Bureau Director Rohit Chopra, and Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services Administrator Chiquita Brooks-LaSure:

Medical debt is one of the leading causes of bankruptcy in the United States with consumers paying more than $433 billion in out-of-pocket healthcare expenses each year.

One of the drivers of out-of-control healthcare costs are medical payment products, including special-purpose credit cards, which have an average interest rate of 27% and installment loans that have hidden finance charges.

I urge the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau to ban predatory medical payment products that take advantage of patients when they are at their most vulnerable, often immediately after a devastating medical diagnosis.

Thank you for putting consumer and patient care over corporate profit, and for your consideration of my comments. Please do NOT add my name to your mailing list. I will learn about future developments on this issue from other sources.

Sincerely,
Christopher Lish
San Rafael, CA

Title: Comment from Blair, Michelle
Comment:

Let’s say I go to the hospital. Luckily I have insurance which pays everything except for 20% of the $1000 (I’m using the simplest denomination here as actual bills from hospitals actually easily cover the cost of a brand new car-something the vast majority of the people our congress represents cannot afford !!) hospital bill.  (mind you, this bill just covers the hospital itself-I will still be bombarded with a bill from each and every Dr., Dr. Ass., Lab Tech, and professional xray, cat scan, whatever scan reader my Drs. have sent something to-(fact)the reason I know this is due to the fact that I questioned my own personal surgeon about bills I had received from people I had NEVER heard of or spoken to pertaining to said hospital surgery(fact)-to formulate or second opinion their own diagnosis.
So I now have a $200 bill after my heath insurance (that I pay out of my gross monthly pay $240 per month for as long as I work at whatever company) has covered it’s share. With 90 days in which to pay it before they send it to a collection agency( with a nonpayment mark on all the current credit agencies) . Once handed over to the collection department it is then added to the balance a large collection fee ( mind you the hospital itself pays the collection agency a fee to try and recover any loss, so if they are able to collect anything -this agency has just doubled its revenue) and my $200 hospital bill now becomes $250. Collection agencies use  cleverly concealed wording within their paper mailed bills stating that if one was to pay just $1 towards the $250 owed bill that in effect is lawful acceptance of said $250 bill which will then be assessed a monthly apr of 10% interest plus a $10 late fee if not paid timely each month= $250$275$285$$313.50$323.50$355.80$365.80in two months!! Within 6 months of receiving a $200 hospital bill I could be looking at paying $365 + for it through a credit agency (has anyone looked up what kind of profits these companies earn? I know for a (fact) my home and auto insurance company has recorded a profit every year for the 30 years I have been their customer(fact) .  Due to unpayment and fees and interest of my ever increasing $200 hospital bill plus bad marks against my credit which renders me unable to receive credit from anywhere and anyone I am left with the lone option of  receiving credit from a medical payment agency (who then takes my $365 bill which in their agreement I must sign will state that I am aware that I owe a total of $365 to the hospital I received service (which is not the case/I owe them $200) and that I agree to their credit terms (which are much like any credit card except either minus the monthly fee, or minus the interest compounded monthly instead of daily. and the process just starts another circle. 
If you personally earn $100,000 per year or more please make the original $200 hospital bill    match your income (as everyone natually gets accustomed with the $they earn and high earners need to see how this scheme would work on themselves in reality to know the full real impact this has on the average person )
and start with a $2000 hospital bill . 
If the hospital receives anything at all from the company they hired to retrieve payment, they receive $200 minus the fee or percentage of amount of payment. This whole system help’s neither hospital or  patient , preys on those who are unable to afford to pay bills they owe to begin with yet legally holds them liable for bill plus incredible costs of increasing fees and interest through time. It preys on those who want to pay bills they owe but (thanks to our public educational system) lack any knowledge of compounding interest either monthly, weekly, daily or hourly with no one to explain it in terms they could understand. 
I hope that you-someone who has the power to control these predators of common people who honestly want to pay the hospital who helped them when they needed it!!

Title: Comment from Stam, Dean
Comment:
I made a regrettable decision when I chose to utilize Care Credit back in the summer of 2021 for a substantial dental procedure. Despite having Delta Dental insurance, it didn't cover the costs of dental crowns. During my consultation with the dentist, he introduced me to the Care Credit financing option, which I had never heard of before. Care Credit materials seemed omnipresent in his office, with posters and pamphlets prominently displayed. Initially, I had intended to charge the dental expenses to my credit cards. However, the dentist's pitch for Care Credit made it sound more appealing than using my Chase card. In fact, he was so persuasive that I half-jokingly suggested he should consider a career promoting Care Credit if he ever tired of dentistry.

I opted for the 24-month financing plan without fully discussing the consequences if I failed to pay off the balance within that timeframe. Unfortunately, the dentist's office lacked a financial expert to provide guidance, and I was left on my own. I was approved for a $20,000 Care Credit limit, and the dentist promptly received his $20,000 payment, even before the dental work began. The work spanned from June to September, and to my astonishment, an additional dental procedure I had in January 2023, costing $1,750, was included in the 24-month plan. This left me baffled and frustrated, as I typically avoided credit cards of this nature due to my mistrust of them.

When the 24-month period concluded, I still had $8,000 remaining on my balance, and Care Credit tacked on an exorbitant $10,000 in interest charges. This financial burden was exacerbated by the challenges my small business faced due to the pandemic and escalating inflation. I had opted for paperless billing, but Care Credit buried the monthly statements under numerous tabs, making it difficult for me to keep track of when the balance needed to be paid off. They also failed to send any reminders, despite bombarding my inbox with countless emails each month promoting various uses of the Care Credit card.

Recently, I returned to the dentist to address issues with two dental crowns. One crown had sustained porcelain damage, while the other was at risk of becoming loose. Although I wasn't satisfied with the previous work, I had no choice but to get them fixed. To add insult to injury, they billed Dental Delta $120 for an x-ray. The Care Credit posters had disappeared from the dentist's office, leaving me to wonder how many others had faced similar challenges using Care Credit. These types of financing programs are indeed fraught with risks, akin to the issues associated with document-less home loans or subprime home loans that have been widely discussed.

If you need more information in the future, let me know. Thank you.

Title: Comment from Despard, Mathieu
Comment:
I wish to applaud the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau for looking into medical payment products. In a study my colleagues and I conducted concerning one of these products ("MedPut"), we found that among employees who registered for MedPut - a credit product and workplace benefit that helps employees pay for medical expenses via interest-free payroll deductions - users had much higher rates of 1) problems paying for medical care; 2) being contacted by a collections agency; 3) putting off needed medical or dental care due to costs; and 4) putting off getting a prescription filled due to costs. Per Section I.a of the of the Request, these results suggest that these credit products may be reaching a very economically vulnerable group of patients who are struggling to pay for health care. In addition, employees who paid a monthly fee to have MedPut were more likely to use the product compared to those whose employer paid the monthly fee, suggesting that credit usage may in part be compelled by a sunk cost fallacy.

We also found that 77% of all bills submitted for payment by MedPut were for delinquent bills. Meanwhile, the aggregate discount rate for which MedPut was able to negotiate was only 18% - discounts health care providers offered primarily on the basis of their financial assistance programs (FAPs; aka "charity care"), though these discounts were unrelated to employees' salaries. Anecdotally, my colleagues and I reviewed the websites of 10 medical credit providers; only one of these (MedPut) made any mention of FAPs and/or attempting to negotiate discounts on behalf of patients. These findings address the Bureau's concern in the Request with respect to whether tax-exempt hospitals are making "reasonable efforts to determine whether the individual is
eligible for assistance under the hospital’s financial assistance policy" and suggest that it is very important to examine the association between health care providers' offers of credit options via 3rd party providers and whether these offers have only been made after patients have been proactively screened for FAP eligibility. This type of research could help inform Internal Revenue Service regulations under Section 501(r)(4) of the Internal Revenue Code (as amended by the Affordable Care Act) with respect to nonprofit hospitals’ FAP requirements and reporting on Schedule H of the Form 990.

Lastly, based on recent research by the Kaiser Family Foundation, I encourage the Bureau to scrutinize the contracts between credit providers and health care providers (mostly hospitals). These contracts have been secured by the Kaiser Family Foundation and the North Carolina State Treasurer's office via Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) requests. Details concerning our study can be found in the attached paper which was published recently in Social Work in Health Care.

Title: Comment from National Disability Institute
Comment:
Re: Request for Information Regarding Specialty Medical Payment Products Consumer Financial Protection Bureau 1700 G Street NW Washington, DC 20552
Assistive technology helps to meet the medical need of a person who has a disability or a medical condition.  A modified vehicle is a form of assistive technology that helps to address the medical needs for those who rely on private transportation to get themselves to their jobs, medical appointments and to commute within their communities.  
The 2017 National Household Travel Survey, indicates that 25.5 million people over age five report disabilities that limit their ability to travel [2]. Of these, 11.6% use a manual wheelchair, 3.9% use power wheelchairs, and 4.4% use scooters, indicating that about 5 million people use wheeled mobility devices in the USA. Many of these people own modified vehicles. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC8835052/
There are limited modified vehicle dealerships nationally that address this volume of assistive technology by providing modified vehicles. There is not significant competition within this industry, and it is thought that price gouging is a common practice among these dealerships. There are reports of modified caravans selling for $120,000. When competition is limited, there is little opportunity for cost comparison. On top of that, many dealers advertise interest at 4%, when in fact, many of the vehicle purchase loans are issued at 18% or higher with repayment terms that may be up to 10 or 15 years. Such selling points and terms make these vehicles unaffordable.
Modified vehicle sites list wheelchair accessible vehicles, that have more than 150,000 miles with modifications having been done more than 10 years ago, starting at a selling price of $40,000 or more. What is the true value of those vehicles that had those modifications completed ten or more years ago? Conventional financing through banks and credit unions is not available for modified vehicles when the blue-book value does not equate to the vehicle’s base value. This AT vendor financing leads to lending terms with interest rates from 18% to 30% with extended repayment terms of up to 10 or 15 years.
Facilities that provide hand-control conversion of vehicles are even more limited. There are hand control assessments and costs that range from $1,200 - $30,000 to $60,000 or more. The limited competition again results in hand control conversion interest rates at 18% or more. Even for people who have credit scores in the range of 720 to 825, interest rates are commonly offered at 18% interest or higher. 
There are also instances when modified vehicles are sold, without reasonable repairs being done prior to sale. Some consumers purchase extended warranties or rely on pre-purchase evaluations in an effort to protect themselves when purchasing a modified vehicle, adding more cost to the purchase. 


Recommendation: There should be a standard appraised value for common vehicle modification features. Modifications that were completed years ago should be subject to depreciation. Kelly Blue Book should address these values within their auto guide; this could help customers know the value of modified vehicles. 

There are many other forms of assistive technology (AT) such as hearing or vision aids that may or may not be included within health care policies. Many times, AT vendors rely on CareCredit or other finance companies that offer 0% interest for up to 6 months. For customers who qualify for this line of credit, who manage to pay on time each of those 6 months, that works out fine. But, if a payment is missed or the loan is not paid in full within 6 months, an interest rate of 18% is applied upon the original loan amount, dated back to the date of loan / credit origination. This practice makes AT, a form of a medical product, unaffordable.

Thank you for the opportunity to submit comments!

Respectfully Submitted, Laurie Schaller
Manager Financial Empowerment
National Disability Institute, Assistive Technology Loan Program / New Jersey and New York

Title: Comment from Hospital Financial Managment Association
Comment:
Dear Comment Intake Team:

The following are five concrete proposals aimed at preventing patients from encountering unaffordable healthcare costs:

1.	Sustain efforts to ensure universal enrollment in comprehensive healthcare coverage for all individuals.
2.	Consider allevaiating providers from the collection of cost-sharing by encouraging health plans, the entities determining enrollees' financial obligations, to directly collect the required cost-sharing amounts. This would significantly reduce patient bills from providers and other care givers.
3.	Restrict the availability of high-deductible health plans to individuals who can demonstrate the capacity to afford the associated cost-sharing.
4.	Prohibit the sale of health sharing ministry products and short-term, limited-duration plans that extend coverage beyond 90 days.
5.	Lower the maximum allowable out-of-pocket cost limits.

Title: Comment from Clark, Jodi
Comment:
Back in late 2012, I was a graduate student in college without any health care or dental care coverage when I had one of my molars collapse on me. I was in excruciating pain and the dentist informed me I would need a root canal and a crown. The dentist recommended that I open a Care Credit card for the $900 it would cost to have the work done. The only other option presented to me at the time was to continue on with my nerve exposed in the molar. I did not know what to do, and I was in so much pain that I couldn't concentrate on my school work. It was necessary to have the root canal and the crown, so I opened the card and charged the procedure to the card. Several months went by with me making very tiny ($30-$50) payments on the card before I had an emergency where I had to rush my cat to the vet for an x-ray. The veterinarian suggested I use Care Credit (again) to pay for the x-ray for my cat. If I did not get the x-ray, there was nothing else the vet could do for me and they suggested I put my companion animal of four years to sleep. I asked for a credit increase on my Care Credit card and was denied. The vet could do nothing further for me, but I asked to be allowed to take my cat home with me after paying the $200 emergency room exam fee, and continued to provide as best I could for my cat even though I could not afford care for her.

About a year or so later, in 2014, I was still paying off the Care Credit card when I had to be hospitalized in an emergency situation. I was in the hospital for five days. I could not put the bill on my Care Credit card, and I was without health insurance, but I needed to pay off the hospital bill. So I ceased paying on my Care Credit and eventually the $650 remaining balance on the card went to collections, where it remained on my credit report for seven years before falling off (as did the hospital bill, but that's another story).

As a graduate student, I dealt with homelessness, a lack of financial security, and a lack of comprehensive care for my disabilities, including the illness that landed me in the hospital for five days. I was preyed upon by these medical payment products that promised to get me the care I needed when I needed it, but failed on two fronts when they charged excessive interest that I could not afford to pay back and when they ceased to provide coverage I needed for emergency situations. Not only was Care Credit predatory in the way they removed the dentist's obligation to aid a young woman in pain, but they also preyed upon me as a poor adult living on less than $1000 a month trying to make my way through school.

These products should not be allowed to charge unregulated interest rates that surprise people with bills they can't pay. These products tank credit scores and destroy futures for young and low income people like I was.
